main_summary.summary: Eddie Holloway, born 1952 in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, was interviewed at the University of Southern Mississippi on December 2, 2015 by Emily Crosby, John Bishop, and Guha Shankar. Holloway describes growing up on Mobile Street, the self-contained Black business district of Hattiesburg, where his mother Beatrice ran Bea's Beauty Salon and his father Willie worked over sixty years at Meridian Fertilizer Company. After his mother died when he was eleven, Holloway spent summers in Bessemer, Alabama with extended family who took him to the 16th Street Baptist Church, instilled civil rights consciousness, and pressed standards of self-reliance. He attended W.H. Jones Elementary and Rowan High School under principal N.R. Burger, whom Holloway frames as a strategic visionary negotiating equal funding within segregated education. Holloway enrolled at USM in 1970, five years after the institution desegregated, became the first African American resident assistant, and remained at USM as Dean of Students. He recounts confronting Dr. A.T. Tatum's segregated medical practice, losing a scholarship for criticizing Senator James O. Eastland, and serving on the Mississippi Civil Rights Museum commission and the Mississippi Coalition Against Sexual Assault.
key_themes: Segregated education and N.R. Burger leadership
Hattiesburg voting rights and Theron Lynd
main_summary.historical_significance:
Holloway provides a first-person ground-level account of Hattiesburg's Mobile Street as a self-contained Black commercial district before urban renewal, identifying specific businesses, professionals, and the catchment-area role it played for surrounding McLain, Beaumont, Richton, and Wiggins communities. He gives one of the canonical insider accounts of N.R. Burger's strategic leadership at Rowan High School, including the contested question of Burger's role in the Clyde Kennard case, drawing on direct conversations with Aubrey Lucas, Burger's son, and Vernon Dahmer's son. His testimony about "the spirit" — the informal Hattiesburg-Black-community enforcement collective that visited boycott-breakers at night — is a first-person source on a practice rarely documented in the secondary literature, distinct from the Deacons for Defense.

Holloway's interview also documents the institutional integration of the University of Southern Mississippi from inside, including his work establishing the office of inclusion, the head residency at Elam Arms, and his testimony to administrators including Aubrey Lucas and Joe Paul. His account of refusing the back-door entry at Dr. A.T. Tatum's office and filing a federal complaint, then receiving Office of Civil Rights consultative calls for twenty-five years, captures the post-1965 phase of Mississippi civil rights work.

chapters.title: Birth and Family in Hattiesburg
chapters.summary: Holloway introduces himself as born September 26, 1952 in Hattiesburg, Mississippi to Beatrice Gibson Holloway from York, Alabama and Willie Holloway, native to Hattiesburg. He describes his father's sixty-year tenure at the Meridian Fertilizer Company (precursor to Coastal Chemicals, home office Yazoo City) beginning at one dollar a day, eventually becoming office custodian and post-retirement watchman.

chapters.title: Mobile Street Community
chapters.summary: Holloway describes 904 Mobile Street, where his mother's Bea's Beauty Salon was attached to their home. He inventories the self-contained Mobile Street commercial district: a movie theater, twelve to fifteen restaurants and clubs, beauty salons and barbershops, a physician's office, dentist, pharmacist, haberdasheries, tailor shops, six cleaners, four churches, a watchmaker, four motels and a hotel, dance halls. The district served a thirty-mile catchment area drawing residents from McLain, Beaumont, Richton, and Wiggins every Saturday.

chapters.title: Schooling at W.H. Jones and Rowan High
chapters.summary: Holloway attended the Maddie Robinson kindergarten, then W.H. Jones Elementary and Junior High (opened 1958), and Rowan High School on Royal Street (later renamed Martin Luther King Avenue). He describes hearing teachers and Mrs. Sip, president of the Mississippi Teachers Association, discuss the building of new Black schools such as W.H. Jones and Lillie Burney as a state strategy to forestall integration after Brown. He explains the parallel MTA structures — Mississippi Teachers Association for African American teachers, MTA for Caucasian teachers — meeting separately in Jackson on different days.

chapters.title: Mother's Death and Alabama Family
chapters.summary: Holloway's mother died when he was eleven, after which his aunt and uncle Earl and Louise from Bessemer, Alabama (twelve miles from Birmingham) became central figures. They were 65 when they took him in, deeply pro-civil-rights and pro-education though formally uneducated. Every summer and holiday Holloway took the train to stay with them. They took him to the 16th Street Baptist Church, the Say Hey Burger restaurant, and the Birmingham Black Barons stadium associated with Willie Mays, instilling standards of pride, presentation, and self-reliance.

chapters.title: N.R. Burger and Black School Equity
chapters.summary: Holloway frames N.R. Burger as a visionary principal at Eureka and Rowan High School who chose Cornell University for graduate work and built a Hattiesburg Black school system focused on equal funding rather than integration. Burger's son and others later confirmed for Holloway that Burger pushed for parity in average daily attendance funding, books, and lab equipment. Holloway recalls receiving one new geometry textbook in October or November 1968 — books typically arrived as hand-me-downs from white schools. Hattiesburg was one of few Mississippi places offering a twelfth-grade Black diploma in the 1940s, drawing students from a fifty-mile radius.

chapters.title: Father's Voting Stance and Downtown Access
chapters.summary: Holloway's father Willie never voted, valuing his standing with white power-holders at the fertilizer company. Willie's network gave Holloway softer treatment at downtown Hattiesburg businesses — Sears, JC Penney, Bill Whitley, the pharmacy, the service station — where his father had accounts. Holloway recalls the wide circulation of Theron Lynd as a topic in Hattiesburg, referencing the book Count Them One by One on the voting rights effort, and the earlier group of fifteen and more who attempted to register.

chapters.title: Mass Meetings and Freedom Schools
chapters.summary: Holloway describes near-nightly mass meetings at True Light Baptist Church, St. Paul, Mount Zion Baptist Church, and Palmer's Crossing. After his mother's death he had more freedom to attend, and one summer relatives from Muskegon, Michigan gave him additional latitude. The mass meetings were his first integrated meetings with whites. He participated in Freedom Schools at the community center on Mobile Street and at True Light Baptist Church, borrowing books and attending some classes on civics, but the Alabama family pressed self-sufficiency over accepting freedom-school food. He marched but withdrew when Klan presence with guns appeared.

chapters.title: Dr. Tatum's Office and Federal Filings
chapters.summary: Holloway confronted Dr. A.T. Tatum's segregated medical practice in the late 1960s or early 1970s, refusing the back-door entry and filing a complaint with the Office of Civil Rights in Washington. Tatum told Holloway he could no longer return to the office. From that point Holloway received Office of Civil Rights consultative calls for twenty-five years on voting location changes and school boundary issues, and filed objections to separate medical facilities at the old Methodist Hospital in Petal, Mississippi.

chapters.title: Sheraton Hotel Scholarship Interview
chapters.summary: Holloway recounts the Sheraton Hotel in Jackson scholarship interview where the interviewer asked his opinion of Senator James O. Eastland. Holloway answered with measured criticism of Eastland's Farm Subsidies Act work as a major landholder paid not to grow crops. The interviewer denied him the scholarship explicitly because Holloway had told the truth without accommodation. Holloway draws from this the lesson of framing and reframing to communicate honestly without being removed from the conversation.

chapters.title: Clyde Kennard and USM
chapters.summary: Holloway recounts thirty years of conversation with Aubrey Lucas, William D. McCain, and N.R. Burger's son about Clyde Kennard's denied entry to USM. Lucas told Holloway that McCain held that Mississippi higher education should be integrated but that USM was one vote away from being closed by the state board, so integration should start at Ole Miss. Lucas also said McCain remarked it would be perfect if Ole Miss were winning the SEC the year integration occurred. Holloway hears the contested account that Burger may have negotiated state funding for the junior college at Piney Woods to keep Kennard out of USM.

chapters.title: USM as Mississippi's Fourth HBCU
chapters.summary: Holloway describes USM's transformation, calling it an inside joke that USM is Mississippi's fourth HBCU with roughly forty percent African American enrollment. He credits J. Ralph Noonkester at William Carey College — the first private Mississippi college to integrate — for collaborating with N.R. Burger, who selected the Rowan High students who integrated in 1965. Holloway worked with Aubrey Lucas and Joe Paul to build USM's Office of Inclusion, civility training, and Office of Student Affairs busman services for high-risk first-generation students from a 120-mile catchment area.

chapters.title: Civil Rights Commission and Museum Work
chapters.summary: Holloway serves on Mississippi's Civil Rights Commission, established by legislative act to integrate civil rights content into public school curriculum, working with the William Winter Institute for Racial Reconciliation, William Winter, and Susan Glisson. He also sits on the Mississippi Coalition Against Sexual Assault. He met with the Civil Rights Museum and Mississippi History Museum planning groups four or five months before the interview. He founded Leadership Hattiesburg twenty-five years prior. He emphasizes that museum exits must leave visitors with a dream rather than only agony.
related_events: Mississippi Civil Rights Museum planning (pre-2017 opening)

chapters.title: The Spirit and Reflections
chapters.summary: Holloway describes 'the spirit' — the unidentified Hattiesburg Black-community enforcement collective that visited boycott-breakers at night, breaking windows or otherwise sanctioning those who crossed economic boycott lines. He distinguishes the spirit from the Deacons for Defense, who provided dignitary security. He references the Ladner sisters Dora and Joyce, the Brown University–Tougaloo partnership, and Joan Trumpauer Mulholland's Tougaloo work. Holloway closes by framing inside-the-system negotiation as the more effective civil rights mode in his experience, advocating institutional accommodation for students bringing academic and social deficits.
